反Vaxxers是“疯子”。这是鲍里斯·约翰逊(Boris Johnson)上周的总结，他参观了伦敦的一家全科医生诊所，讨论扩大流感疫苗的覆盖范围。在政府对流感大流行发表含糊其辞的建议的时候，这是一个令人耳目一新的清晰时刻。
疫苗确实起到了作用，拯救了数百万人的生命-事实上，也许是医学界最具讽刺意味的事情是，人类已知的最有效、最有益的干预措施之一，却被认为是最不信任和最具猜疑的干预措施之一。对于首相，我们可能会问：为什么这些顽固的反疫苗傻瓜就不能相信专家呢？
但对反疫苗运动持这种非黑即白的看法可能是不明智的。因为尽管关于疫苗有效性和安全性的证据是明确的，但科学家和医生多年来在处理疫苗时的失误和错误却丝毫不能幸免。让古怪、墨守成规的反避孕者与冷静、无所不知的专家对立起来，可能会掩盖这些专家过去所犯的错误-而且无法从警示故事中吸取教训。
上周是疫苗的重要一周。除了政府推动流感疫苗的努力外，两项新的新冠肺炎疫苗试验的初步结果也发表在《柳叶刀》杂志上-一项来自英国，另一项来自中国。他们加入了前一周在美国进行的一项试验，以证明他们的候选疫苗具有可耐受的副作用，并且肯定会引起免疫反应。这些第一阶段和第二阶段试验只是在寻找有效疫苗的道路上的第一步-尽管是至关重要的-步骤。接下来，我们进入更大规模的第三阶段试验，涉及数千人，测试那些接种疫苗的人是否真的比那些注射安慰剂的人感染新冠肺炎的可能性更小。
特别是“柳叶刀”带头宣传新冠肺炎疫苗的研究，确实让人感到有点讽刺：除了是一本超级享有盛誉的医学杂志外，这本杂志的主要公众知名度还在于发表了有史以来最糟糕和最具破坏性的疫苗研究之一。当然，这是安德鲁·韦克菲尔德在“柳叶刀”上发表的一篇臭名昭著的文章，将MMR疫苗与自闭症联系起来。它在1998年的出现激起了反疫苗运动的火上浇油，媒体对MMR产生了毁灭性的怀疑-由此导致英国和世界范围内麻疹病例的致命激增。
自那以后，许多大型、高质量的研究发现，韦克菲尔德的MMR-自闭症相关性以及他关于疫苗减毒病毒如何导致肠道和大脑相关症状的夸张理论是不真实的。然而，更糟糕的是，维克菲尔德不仅仅是错的-他是一个骗子。他捏造了他研究中每个孩子的医疗记录，让他们的自闭症症状看起来就像是在MMR之后不久出现的，而许多人在接种疫苗之前或很长一段时间之后就出现了症状。
韦克菲尔德论文的发表将永远是“柳叶刀”和更广泛的科学同行评议过程中的一个污点。即使没有欺诈，一项只包括12名患者的具有如此惊天动地影响的研究也不应该在如此突出的地方发表。使损害雪上加霜的是，该报被正式撤回所需的时间太长--令人震惊的12年之久。在此期间，反Vaxxers可以指出同行评议的研究-在“柳叶刀”上，同样如此！-来支持他们的观点。
韦克菲尔德正在推开一扇门，虽然可能没有完全打开，但肯定是半开的。反疫苗运动已经伴随我们很长一段时间了--事实上，和疫苗一样长。一些著名的历史科学家已经参与其中：例如，阿尔弗雷德·罗素·华莱士(Alfred Russel Wallace)提出了独立于查尔斯·达尔文(Charles Darwin)的自然选择的想法，他在19世纪末是天花疫苗的顽固反对者。
虽然华莱士肯定有点“疯狂”-他花了大量时间试图说服他的同龄人，灵媒真的可以与死者交谈，并为鬼魂拍照-但他分析疫苗效果的方法(他认为疫苗是非常危险的)在当时的统计上是相当复杂的。考虑到统计数据还处于发展的早期阶段，支持疫苗的一方几乎没有更令人信服的数据来反对他。
华莱士还在反家长主义(他写道，强制疫苗法是对个人自由和家庭神圣的粗暴干涉)甚至社会主义方面表达了他的反对，声称疫苗被视为一种速效药物，未能“摆脱”导致疾病在维多利亚时代英国穷人中传播的肮脏条件。当然，这些论点是完全错误的，但它们并不是疯子的胡言乱语，它们暗示了反疫苗立场的复杂性：疫苗的捍卫者需要在多条战线上工作，以打击绝不容易被揭穿的反对意见。
心理学家喜欢引用一系列认知偏差来解释为什么人们对疫苗不理性：可获得性启发式(人们过于关注几个可怕的故事，而忽视了对情况的更理性评估)、遗漏偏差(即做了一件事，比如给孩子接种疫苗，如果出了什么问题，感觉比什么都没做更糟糕)，等等。
但最近的证据似乎表明有一个更简单的原因在起作用：如果人们认为他们很有可能后悔接种疫苗，他们就不太可能这么做。很少有证据表明我们可以改变人们对疫苗的看法，让他们变得更好；但通过提供听起来更可信的后悔理由(比如韦克菲尔德的MMR-自闭症联系)，让人们变得更坏要容易得多。
唉，韦克菲尔德研究报告的发表远不是疫苗科学家和监管者多年来唯一的重大失误。在他的虚假论文发表后不久，美国和欧盟在硫柳汞(有时拼写为硫柳汞)问题上向反疫苗活动人士投降。硫柳汞是一种含汞的化学物质，长期以来一直被用作疫苗的防腐剂，而且--你猜到了--反疫苗者将其与自闭症联系在一起。
给孩子的手臂注射汞的想法听起来可能非常可怕，但硫柳汞(乙基汞)中的汞类型与我们通常讨论的汞中毒类型(甲基汞，它在血液中停留的时间要长得多，即使是小剂量的甲基汞也可能有严重的破坏性影响)不同。从来没有任何证据支持活动人士的断言，即含有硫柳汞的疫苗与自闭症有关，但一旦卫生专家突然决定必须取消硫柳汞，从本世纪之交开始，几乎所有给儿童接种的疫苗都禁止使用硫柳汞，似乎就肯定发生了什么。
这些权威人士开始直言不讳地说，硫柳汞是完全安全的，并建议应该“尽快”将其从疫苗中移除。家长们应该怎么想呢？反疫苗活动家再次利用这种混乱来强化他们的信息，在疫苗中不必要地移除防腐剂后，汞自闭症争议的爆发-以及法庭上过多的人身伤害索赔-发生了。
专家们不仅将其他仍在疫苗中使用硫柳汞的国家-特别是发展中国家-置于令人反感的境地，他们还用鱼雷破坏了自己未来的权威。任何使用“完全安全”或“无不良反应证据”的…。现在会被提及他们在硫柳汞问题上似乎缺乏直截了当的态度。
这段疫苗接种的历史提供了许多进一步的例子。1976年，流感疫苗在美国匆忙、政治化地推出--以及它可能导致的罕见神经疾病格林-巴利综合征(Guillain-Barrésyndrome)的增加--被认为是引发反疫苗怀疑的罪魁祸首；1955年，灾难性的实验室失误导致20万儿童注射脊髓灰质炎活病毒，导致数百人永久瘫痪，10人死亡。
尽管安全程序已经大幅改善，但灾难仍然发生，比如2016年菲律宾登革热疫苗有问题的案例，最终导致病情恶化。其他的失败不是医生或科学家的错，但仍然产生了糟糕的疫苗宣传：例如，谁会忘记中央情报局(CIA)用来刺探奥萨马·本·拉登(Osama Bin Laden)秘密院落并获取他家人DNA的假疫苗接种计划？
那么，你必须是“疯子”才会在疫苗问题上犹豫不决吗？当然，你必须有点脱离现实，才能相信比尔·盖茨只是在帮助资助新冠肺炎疫苗的开发，这样他就可以给我们所有人注射微芯片。要想成为一名反Vaxxer，你必须对疫苗接种对人类福祉近乎奇迹般的效果的证据一无所知。但是，在试图说服那些不是这场运动的铁杆成员的过程中，科学家、医生和政治家需要从那些事情没有按计划进行的历史先例清单中吸取教训。
这些教训是显而易见的，但没有得到重视。我们不能低估新冠肺炎疫苗的任何副作用。我们不能提供听起来自信而又自相矛盾的建议-就像我们在这次大流行期间已经对口罩所做的那样，首先建议它们毫无用处，甚至可能是危险的(这可能是也可能不是为医护人员保存口罩库存的“高尚谎言”)，然后强制在商店和公共交通工具中使用口罩。我们不能相信阴谋论。最重要的是，我们必须诚实和透明地了解疫苗是如何起作用的，谁是优先获得疫苗的人，何时获得疫苗，以及我们最终在人口中推广的目标是什么。
当新冠肺炎疫苗到来时--我们不要屏息，但早期迹象是有希望的--它将加入有史以来最重要的医学发现的行列。让我们利用过去的失败为自己接种疫苗，防止未来的错误-并确保疫苗成为我们需要的大流行拦截器。
因此，多米尼克·卡明斯，一位知名人物，尽管身为首相的首席政治顾问相当忙碌，但还是不得不自己开车送货？如果他们想收集一大箱有关疫苗的文件，难道他们不能刚刚使用皇家邮政吗？如果你打算在……制造一个阴谋论。多读。
为了进一步讨论这篇文章，我对医疗机构计划给儿童接种快速实验性冠状病毒疫苗产品以保护老年人的计划提出了质疑。我认为给儿童接种疫苗是不道德的，因为这种病毒对…。多读。
它可以帮助患有Covid的患者的机制是众所周知的。
但这并不管用。这是这里的底线。经过测试，发现是想要的，为甚麽大家还在喋喋不休地说这件破了的不治疗的事呢？多读
note_en:
Anti-vaxxers are “nuts”. That was Boris Johnson’s   summation  last week, as he visited a London GP surgery to discuss expanding coverage of the flu vaccine. In a time of mealy-mouthed advice from the government on the pandemic, this was a refreshing moment of clarity.
 Vaccines really do work, and have saved millions of lives — indeed, it’s perhaps the biggest irony in medicine that one of the most effective and beneficial interventions known to humanity is the one that’s regarded with the most mistrust and suspicion. With the Prime Minister, we might ask: why can’t these recalcitrant anti-vaccine fools just trust the experts?
 But it might be unwise to take such a black-and-white view of the anti-vaccination movement. For although the evidence on the effectiveness and safety of vaccines is clear, scientists and doctors have been anything but immune to missteps and errors in how they’ve dealt with them over the years. Pitting the wacky, hidebound anti-vaxxers against the sober, all-knowing experts risks glossing over the mistakes those experts have made in the past — and failing to learn from the cautionary tales.
 Last week was a big week for vaccines. As well as the government’s flu vaccine push, initial results from two new COVID-19 vaccine trials appeared in the journal   The Lancet  — one from the   UK  and one from   China . They join a   US trial  from the week before in showing that their candidate vaccines have tolerable side-effects and definitely do cause an immune response. These Phase I and Phase II trials are just the first — albeit crucial — steps on the way to finding a working vaccine. Next, we move to larger, Phase III trials that involve many thousands of people, testing whether those who get the vaccine are actually less likely to catch Covid-19 than those who are injected with a placebo.
 It does feel somewhat ironic that   The Lancet  in particular is taking the lead in publicising the research on Covid-19 vaccines: besides being a super-prestigious medical journal, the main public claim to fame of that particular journal is in publishing one of the worst and most damaging vaccine studies of all time. That was, of course, Andrew Wakefield’s notorious   Lancet   article  linking the MMR vaccine to autism. Its appearance in 1998 fanned the flames of the anti-vaccine movement, with crushing media suspicion falling on the MMR — and a resulting deadly surge in measles cases in the UK and worldwide.
  Many large, high-quality   studies  have since found that Wakefield’s MMR-autism correlation — as well as his cockamamie theory about how the attenuated virus from the vaccine causes gut- and brain-related symptoms — is untrue. Worse, though, is the fact that Wakefield wasn’t just wrong — he was a fraud. He   fabricated  the medical records of   every single one  of the children in his study to make it appear as if their autism symptoms had come just after the MMR, whereas many had shown symptoms before, or a long time after, receiving the vaccine.
 The publication of the Wakefield paper will forever be a stain on   The Lancet , and on the peer-review process of science more generally. Even without the fraud, a study with such earth-shattering implications that only included twelve patients should never have been published somewhere so prominent. Compounding the damage was the sheer amount of time it took for the paper to be officially retracted – an astonishing twelve years. All the while, anti-vaxxers could point to a peer-reviewed study — in   The Lancet , no less! — to support their view.
 Wakefield was pushing at a door that, while perhaps not fully open, was certainly ajar. The anti-vaccination movement has been with us for a long time — indeed, as long as vaccines. Some renowned historical scientists have been a part of it: for example, Alfred Russel Wallace, who came up with the idea of natural selection independently of Charles Darwin, was an implacable opponent of the smallpox vaccine during the late 19 th  Century.
 Whereas Wallace was certainly a bit “nuts” — he spent a great deal of time trying to convince his peers that spiritualist mediums really could talk to the dead and take photographs of ghosts — his approach to analysing the effects of vaccines (he argued they were actively dangerous) was statistically   quite sophisticated  for the time. And given that statistics were in such an early phase of their development, the pro-vaccine side hardly had much more convincing data to oppose him.
 Wallace also   expressed  his opposition in terms of anti-paternalism (he wrote that compulsory vaccine laws were “a gross interference with personal liberty and the sanctity of the home”) and even socialism, claiming that vaccines were seen as a quick fix that failed to “get rid of the foul conditions” that caused disease to spread among the poor of Victorian Britain. These arguments were, of course, completely wrong-headed, but they weren’t the ravings of a madman, and they hint at the complexity of the anti-vaccine position: defenders of vaccines need to work on multiple fronts to combat   objections  that are anything but easily-debunked.
 Psychologists are fond of invoking a whole series of cognitive biases to explain why people are irrational about vaccines: the availability heuristic (where people focus too much on a few scary stories at the expense of a more rational appraisal of the situation), omission bias (where having committed an act, like getting your child vaccinated, feels worse if something goes wrong than having not done anything), and so on.
 But   more recent evidence  seems to suggest a simpler reason is at play: if people think there’s a high chance they’ll regret vaccination, they’re less likely to do it. Evidence that we can change people’s beliefs about vaccines for the better is   scarce ; but changing them for the worse by providing more credible-sounding reasons for regret — like Wakefield’s MMR-autism connection — is much easier.
  Alas, the publication of Wakefield’s study is far from the only significant fumble by vaccine scientists and regulators over the years. Relatively soon after his phony paper was published, the US and the EU capitulated to anti-vaccine campaigners on the issue of thiomersal (sometimes spelled thimerosal), a mercury-containing chemical that had long been used as a preservative in vaccines, and had been — you guessed it — linked to autism by the anti-vaxxers.
 The idea of injecting mercury into your child’s arm can be made to sound very frightening, but the type of mercury in thiomersal (ethylmercury) isn’t the same as the type we normally discuss with reference to mercury poisoning (methylmercury, which stays in the bloodstream for far longer and can have seriously damaging effects even at small doses). There was never any   evidence  to support campaigners’ assertions that vaccines containing thiomersal were linked to autism, but something certainly   appeared  to be going on once the health experts suddenly decided that thiomersal had to go, banning it from almost all vaccines given to children from around the turn of the century.
 Those   authorities  began speaking out of both sides of their mouths, at once saying thiomersal was completely safe and   also  recommending it should be removed from vaccines “  as expeditiously as possible ”. What were parents supposed to think? Again, anti-vaccine campaigners used the confusion to boost their message, with a flare-up of the mercury-autism controversy — as well as a plethora of personal injury claims in courts — occurring   after  the preservative had needlessly been removed from the vaccines.
 Not only did the experts put other countries that still used thiomersal in their vaccines — particularly those in the developing world — in an invidious position, they also torpedoed their own future authority. Any use of “completely safe” or “no evidence for adverse effects…” would now be met with references to their seeming lack of straightforwardness on thiomersal.
 This history of vaccination gives numerous further examples. The   rushed, politicised rollout  of the flu vaccine in the US in 1976 — and the potential increase it caused in the rare neurological condition Guillain-Barré Syndrome — has been blamed for spawning anti-vaccine suspicion; as have the   catastrophic lab errors  that caused live polio viruses to be injected into 200,000 children in 1955, permanently paralysing hundreds and killing ten.
 Although safety procedures have since improved dramatically, disasters do still occur, such as the 2016 case of the faulty   dengue fever vaccine  in the Philippines which ended up making the illness worse. Other failures are not the fault of doctors or scientists, but still produce terrible PR for vaccines: who could forget, for example, the   fake vaccination programme  used by the CIA to spy on Osama bin Laden’s secret compound and get his family’s DNA?
  So do you have to be “nuts” to hesitate about vaccines? Certainly, you have to be   somewhat  detached from reality to believe,   as some do , that Bill Gates is only helping fund the development of a Covid-19 vaccine so he can inject us all with microchips. And to be an anti-vaxxer, you have to be grossly ignorant of the evidence for the near-miraculous effects of vaccination on human wellbeing. But in seeking to persuade those who aren’t hardcore members of the movement, scientists, doctors, and politicians need to learn the lessons from the list of historical precedents where things haven’t gone to plan.
 Those lessons are obvious, but underappreciated. We mustn’t   underplay any side-effects  of the Covid-19 vaccine. We mustn’t provide confident-sounding yet contradictory advice — as we already have during this pandemic in the case of facemasks, first advising that they were useless and maybe even dangerous (which may or may not have been a “noble lie” to preserve mask stocks for healthcare workers), then mandating their use in shops and on public transport. We mustn’t give any credibility to conspiracy theories. And we must above all be honest and transparent about how the vaccine works, who is prioritised to get it and when, and what our targets are for its eventual rollout across the population.
 When the Covid-19 vaccine comes — and let’s not hold our breaths, but the early signs are promising — it’ll join the ranks of the most important medical discoveries of all time. Let’s use the failures of the past to inoculate ourselves against errors in the future — and ensure that the vaccine becomes the pandemic-stopper we need it to be.

note:
“就好书而言，关键不是看你能读懂多少本，而是看你能读懂多少本。”-- Mortimer J.Adler。
学习和持续发展是PEX织物的一部分。不足为奇的是，实现这一目标的一种常见方式是读书。我很肯定我不需要说服你读书的好处。不过，我想谈谈我们的读书会，并有可能说服你在工作中尝试一下。因为阅读是好的，但是为了讨论而阅读更好。
好的!。事情是这样的：如果你从来没有参加过读书俱乐部，读书俱乐部听起来可能很书呆子，甚至令人生畏。你可能认为一群人聚在一起讨论一本他们本来就不一定想读的书。但是，听我说完！公司读书会可能更有趣(嗯，…。用小写的“f”)比你想象的要有趣)，这可能对你的职业生活的许多方面和你的公司都有好处。
“嗯，…。你觉得你能从中得到什么呢？“。
和一群人一起读一本共同的书的最大好处之一就是聚在一起讨论它。这是一个学习他人观点的机会。人们关注的东西不同。它们以不同的方式与一本书的各个方面联系在一起，因为我们的个人经历创造了独特的视角。
“这是有道理的，但我从来没想过一个阅读小组会影响阅读体验。”
我认为为了讨论而读书是一种不同的阅读方式。当你知道你要讨论这本书时，它会改变你的关注点，你的阅读会变得更有味道，更令人满意。
当我开始在网上评论书籍时，我第一次注意到阅读体验的变化，读书俱乐部在这方面也很相似；它们让你的阅读更有目的性，让你欣赏到原本可能没有注意到的细微差别。这不一定是关于使用公式或采用科学的分析方法。这更像是停下来问“为什么？”或者“如果呢？”当你注意到一条小线连接在主线上时。扯上这条你可能根本没有注意到的细线，会激发你的好奇心。
根据你正在阅读的内容，这些古董有不同的形状。当你读小说时，你会注意人物发展或情节情节。在非小说类书籍中，比如我们在管理和领导力读书会上阅读的那些书籍，你会关注其他方面，比如思想的适用性、与你的经历进行比较等。总而言之，这是一种更令人满意的阅读体验。
“我喜欢这一点。当你读一本读书俱乐部的书时，有没有什么不同的地方？“。
您可以通过试验和错误来开发自己的技术。实际上，它更像是“试验，然后是👍或👎。例如，我读书时会做笔记。如果我以电子方式阅读，我会突出显示。当我读纸质书时，我会在记事本上做笔记。(是的，我知道！我是个老派。)。笔记让我可以回到它们来收集我的想法，以便进行回顾或即将进行的讨论。有笔记还能鼓励你更好地学习和记忆所需的重复。
一般来说，知道如何阅读一本书是很重要的。作为开场白，我推荐阅读阿德勒和范多伦的“如何阅读一本书”(How To Read A Book)。书中提出的观点可能会改变你的阅读风格。
“听起来不错。听到别人谈论同一本电子书，一定很有意思吧。
一点儿没错!。读书俱乐部也是一个更好地了解你的同事的机会。你会花几个小时讨论一些比随意话题更深入的话题，比如天气或当地的运动队。我们都是由我们以前的经历塑造的，所以我们从一本书中学到的东西几乎从来都不一样。因为读书俱乐部更多的是倾听和欣赏各种观点，而不是讲述你的经历，所以你会成为更好的倾听者和沟通者。
每个月一次，你有机会分析一本书的内容，形成你的想法，并将它们公之于众。这些观点构成了一个小窗口，通过这些窗口，你可以一窥同事的个性和思维过程。作为管理者，了解与你共事的人是非常重要的。
等一等!。还有更多：在专业的日常生活中，普通书籍创造了共同的参考点。当你使用书友会书中的一个比喻或故事时，小组中的每个人都能理解背后的意思。这发生在我最近与人力资源部的一次会议上，我们在会上提到了我们在一本书中读到的积极倾听技巧。
读书俱乐部让你们聚在一起，像一个团队一样思考。你们一起分析，在彼此的想法基础上建立想法，并讨论它们在小型问题解决会议中的适用性。一旦你有了几本常见的书，你也会开始交叉引用某些想法，并讨论重叠或矛盾的部分。
作为一个团队，我们不会严格地促进讨论，也不会在我们的讨论中设定严格的界限；我们会让他们跳来跳去，包括其他相关的话题。我们经常被提醒，我们是在一个安全的环境中工作的，在这种环境中，即使想法和观点导致不同意见，也会鼓励分享。
大部分时间。与小组一起阅读会让你觉得按时完成它是有责任的。它创造了一种节奏。它会产生正确的压力来防止拖延症。但我的第一条规则总是：生命太短暂，有太多好书要读。因此，如果因为某些原因我不喜欢我正在读的书，我会在给它一个公平的机会后把它扔掉。不过，在我们的读书会里，这还没有发生。
“你也可能最终读到一本你本来不会选的书。”
是真的。但即便如此，也有其自身的好处。它把你带出你的舒适区，可以把一本出人意料的好书交到你手中。我们现在读的这本书里有一句很棒的话，让我想到了这一点：
我们需要保持开放的心态，才能学习和欣赏。所以，当你加入读书俱乐部时，思想开放是很重要的。
我们读了各种各样的书。让我简要地告诉你我们最近读过的几本书。
我们的上一本书是苏珊·凯恩的“安静：内向者在无法停止说话的世界中的力量”。我们目前正在阅读大卫·爱泼斯坦的“范围”。这两本书都很有影响力。他们让你了解你的人民和我们生活的世界，让你成为更好的领导者和管理者。
“安静”告诉我们，在某些情况下，与外向的人相比，内向的人有显著的优势，但西方文化将外向的性格理想化，因此低估内向的人会让我们损失很大。
“牧场”是另一本发人深省的书。它告诉我们，我们生活的世界充满了邪恶的意想不到的事件，及早开始并专门化并不能给我们在这样一个世界中创造和创新所需的知识广度。这是另一本书，它告诉我们，我们在表现、成功和教育方面的本能会极大地误导我们。
在“安静”之前，我们阅读了托马斯·戈登的“家长效能培训(P.E.T.)”。顾名思义，主要观众是家长。然而，当你阅读这本书时，你会明白书中提出的想法和方法适用于生活的许多方面，包括职业生活。积极倾听、处理阻力、处理冲突和解决价值冲突等都是非常宝贵的技能。
“我被卖了！但办一个读书会有多容易呢？“。
读书会并不总是容易顺利和成功地运作。人们喜欢这个想法，并对它感到兴奋， - 他们准备跳上船，选择第一本书读。但有时现实与预期不符。人们总是拖拖拉拉的。他们认为床边有更好的书在向他们眨眼。他们觉得自己在读一本他们不一定会自己挑选的书。越来越多的借口堆积如山。这个团体减少了，读书俱乐部默默地消亡了。
不用谢。但是，知道读书会需要开放的心态和关心是件好事。就像生活中的大多数事业一样，知道你为什么要做这件事，以及你想从中得到什么，这是一件很好的事情。
此外，在专业背景下组织的读书俱乐部略有不同。诚然，就其性质而言，加入这样的俱乐部或保持其生存的压力可能会更大。然而，如果你的公司文化给你的员工自由选择不参加这样的活动，那么应该不会有问题。参与在任何阶段都应该是可选的。
目前，我们在PEX有四个读书俱乐部：管理和领导、产品、商业和工程。在管理和领导力读书会上，我们每个月读一本书，然后见面讨论亮点。
我们有一个在线频道，在那里我们可以推荐和讨论要阅读的书籍。入围的书最终会出现在一份名单中，我们可以从中选择下一本书。我们的书单增长缓慢，我们每2-3个月刷新一次投票，以保持榜首的相关性。我们决定集体阅读下一本书，并用我们每月的津贴购买我们的个人拷贝(在PEX，每个员工都有每月津贴来购买书籍 - 以及其他费用，比如支付上班的通勤费)。
简而言之，读书有很多好处，一个经营良好的读书俱乐部可以有更多的好处，比如更好地欣赏一本书，或者成为更好的沟通者。如果你在工作中成立读书俱乐部，其中一些好处甚至可以对你的职业生活和公司产生直接的、积极的影响。
note_en:
“In the case of good books, the point is not to see how many of them you can get through, but rather how many can get through to you.”— Mortimer J. Adler
  Learning and continuous development are part of  Pex’s fabric. Not surprisingly, a common way to achieve this is by reading books. I’m pretty sure that I don’t have to convince you of the benefits of reading books. However, I’d like to talk about our book clubs, and potentially convince you to give it a try at work. Because reading is good, but reading in order to discuss is even better.
    OK! Here’s the thing: If you’ve never been part of one, book clubs may sound nerdy or even intimidating. You might think that a bunch of people get together to discuss a book that they didn’t even necessarily want to read in the first place. But, hear me out! A company book club may be more fun (well… “fun” with a lowercase “f”) than you think, and it may be beneficial to many aspects of your professional life and to your company.
  “Hmm… what do you think you’re getting out of it?”
  One of the great benefits of reading a common book with a group is getting together to discuss it. It is an opportunity to learn from the others’ point of views. People pay attention to different things. They relate to various aspects of a book in diverse ways, because our individual experiences create unique perspectives.
  “Makes sense, but I never thought a reading group could influence the reading experience.”
  I think reading a book in order to discuss it is a different style of reading. When you know that you’re going to discuss the book, it changes your focus, and your reading becomes more flavorful and more satisfying.
  I first noticed the change in my reading experience when I started to review books online, and book clubs are similar in this regard; they make your reading more purposeful, and make you appreciate nuances that you might not otherwise have noticed. It’s not necessarily about using a formula or employing a scientific analysis method. It’s more like stopping and asking “why?” or “what if?” when you notice a small thread attached to the main line. It tickles your curiosity to pull on this thin thread that you might not even have noticed otherwise.
  Depending on what you’re reading, these curiosities have different shapes. When you’re reading fiction, you pay attention to things like character development or plot lines. In non-fiction books, like the ones we’re reading in our management and leadership book club, you pay attention to other aspects, like applicability of the ideas, drawing parallels with your experiences, etc. All in all, it is a much more satisfying reading experience.
  “I like that. Is there anything that you do differently when you read a book-club book?”
  You develop your own techniques by trial and error. Actually it’s more like “trial followed by 👍 or 👎. For example, I take notes when I read. If I read electronically, I highlight. When I read paper books, I take notes on a notepad. (Yes, I know! I’m old school.) Notes allow me to go back to them to gather my thoughts for a review or for the upcoming discussion. Having notes also encourages the repetition that you need to learn and remember better.
  In general, it’s important to know how to read a book. As a starter, I would recommend reading Adler and Van Doren’s “ How To Read A Book.” The ideas presented in it may change your reading style.
  “Sounds good. It must be interesting to hear others talk about the same book.”
  Exactly! A book club is also an opportunity to get to know your colleagues better. You take a few hours to discuss things that run deeper than casual topics such as the weather or the local sports teams. We are all shaped by our previous experiences, so our takeaways from a book are almost never the same. And because a book club is more about listening and appreciating the various points of view than telling about your experience, you become a better listener and a better communicator.
  Once a month, you get an opportunity to analyze the content of a book, form your thoughts, and present them in public. Those points of view constitute small windows through which you can catch a glimpse of your colleagues’ personalities and thought processes. And as managers, it is very important to know the people with whom you work.
    Hold on! There’s more: In professional day-to-day, common books create common reference points. When you use an analogy or a story drawn from a book-club book, everyone in the group can understand the meaning behind it. This happened to me recently in a meeting with HR where we mentioned active listening techniques that we read about in a book.
  Book Club lets you get together and think as a group. You analyze together, build ideas on top of each others’, and discuss their applicability in mini problem solving sessions. Once you have a few common books under your belt, you also start to cross reference certain ideas and discuss parts that are overlapping or contradicting.
  As a group, we don’t apply strict facilitation and set rigid boundaries around our discussions; we let them jump around and include other related topics. We are regularly reminded that  we work in a safe environment where sharing ideas and opinions are encouraged even when they lead to disagreements.
    Most of the time. Reading with a group makes you feel accountable to finish it on time. It creates a cadence. It creates the right type of pressure to prevent procrastination. But my first rule is always: life is too short, and there are too many good books to be read. Therefore, if for some reason I don’t enjoy the book that I’m reading, I will drop it after giving it a fair chance. This has yet to happen in our book clubs, though.
  “You may also end up reading a book that you would not have otherwise picked.”
  True. But even that has its own benefits. It takes you out of your comfort zone and can put a surprisingly good book in your hands. There is an excellent quote in the book we’re currently reading, which made me think of this point:
    We need to keep an open mind to be able to learn and appreciate. So, open-mindedness is important when you join a book club.
    We read a wide variety of books. Let me tell you briefly about the last few books that we’ve read.
  Our last book was “ Quiet: The Power of Introverts in a World That Can’t Stop Talking” by Susan Cain. We are currently reading   “Range ” by David Epstein. They are both very influential books. They make you a better leader and manager by making you understand your people and the world that we live in.
  “Quiet” teaches us that introverts have significant strengths compared to extroverts in certain situations, but that Western culture idealizes extrovert traits, therefore we are losing a lot by undervaluing introverts.
  “Range ” is another thought-provoking book. It teaches us that the world we live in is full of wickedly unanticipated events, and that starting and specializing early doesn’t give us the necessary breadth of knowledge to be creative and innovative in such a world. This is another book that shows us that our instincts in performance, success, and education can mislead us immensely.
  Before “Quiet, ” we read Thomas Gordon’s “ Parental Effectiveness Training (P. E. T.).” As its title indicates, the main audience is parents. However, when you read the book, you understand that the ideas and approaches presented in the book are applicable to many aspects of life, including professional life. Things like active listening, dealing with resistance, handling conflict, and resolving value collisions are invaluable skills to have.
  “I’m sold! But how easy is it to run a book club?”
  Book clubs are not always easy to run smoothly and successfully. People love the idea and get excited about it  —  they are ready to jump onboard and select the first book to read. But sometimes the reality doesn’t match the expectations. People procrastinate. They think there are better books winking at them from the bedside. They feel like they are reading a book that they wouldn’t necessarily have picked on their own. More and more excuses pile up. The group dwindles and the book club dies silently.
    Not at all. But, it’s good to know that book clubs require open-mindedness and care. And just like in most undertakings in life, it’s good to know why you’re doing it and what you want to get out of it.
  Additionally, book clubs organized in a professional setting are slightly different. It is true that, by its nature, there could be more pressure to join such a club or to keep it alive. However, if your company culture gives your people the freedom to opt out of such activities, then there shouldn’t be a problem. Participation should be optional at any stage.
    Currently we have four book clubs at Pex: management and leadership, product, business, and engineering. In the management and leadership book club, we read one book every month and then meet to discuss the highlights.
  We have an online channel where we recommend and discuss books to read. The shortlisted books end up in a list from which we choose our next book. Our book list grows slowly, and we refresh our votes every 2–3 months to keep the top of the list relevant. We decide on the next book to read as a group, and use our monthly allowance to buy our individual copy (at Pex, every employee has a monthly allowance to buy books — among other things like covering the cost of commuting into work).
  In short, reading books has many benefits, and a well-run book club can have many more, like simply better appreciating a book, or becoming a better communicator. And if you start a book club at work, some of these benefits can even have a direct, positive impact on your professional life and company.
title: 读书很好。为了讨论而阅读更好
title_en: Reading is good. Reading in order to discuss is better

note:
这是我最喜欢的USDS故事之一：在我在USDS的职业生涯中，我很快就建立了一个可以发出SOS求救信号的人的声誉，不是在USDS内部，而是在外部与承包商和不同的政府合作伙伴。政府中有相当多的暗箭伤人，甚至在公务员中也是如此。每当你选择向第三方寻求帮助时，你就是在冒着被你联系的人把你搞砸的风险。华盛顿没有宫殿并不意味着我们没有宫殿阴谋。
不管怎样，有一天，这群安全研究人员联系了我，告诉我与他们合作的机构正积极成为国内白人至上主义者的目标。他们已经通知了适当的人，并提出了建议，但却在四处走动。白人至上主义者感兴趣的目标网站有数千个已知的安全漏洞，但它也有一个非常具体的推出日期，没有商量余地，并已写入法律(这在政府中是愚蠢的常见事情)。领导层不想停止解决安全问题的开发工作，但他们也不能被视为忽视了安全问题。尤其是数以千计的人。因此，他们会与研究人员接触，通过会议、实地考察和PPT幻灯片来展示这场盛大的表演，然后什么都不做。
他们在多条战线上这样做，外部顾问和政府监督机构都走得很慢。在这种情况下，我们不能向联邦政府中负责信息安全的众多组织中的一个报告问题。每个可以被要求施加压力的人都已经意识到了情况，并施加了最大的压力。
我建议我们让FBI知道，看看他们是否能在不担心网站的情况下为我们阻止潜在的攻击者。到目前为止还没有犯罪，所以从技术上讲，联邦调查局无法对这些威胁采取任何行动。但他们确实追踪了这类信息，也许安全研究人员掌握的信息可以支持其他调查。我花了几个星期的时间才找到合适的联系人并联系到他们。在确定他是相关特别工作组的成员，他们确实追踪了这一信息，并有兴趣了解我们的发现后，我建议我们去喝杯咖啡，填写一些细节。我已经提供了一组潜在的地点，包括机构自助餐厅，它非常不错。
“实际上，你们有没有我们可以使用的办公室或会议室？”
当然可以，我说。这并没有让我感到困扰，因为“我们去喝咖啡”这句话总是有点比喻的意思。首先，我不喝咖啡。
我在USDS的很大一部分工作是咖啡约会。我们打击官僚主义的方式是建立目标一致、技能或资源兼容的人员网络。与新节点的第一次联系总是非正式的。我需要评估你，确定你的动机，你对风险的容忍度，你是如何定义成功的。但我也需要建立信任。我从来没有想过要以任何不同的方式处理这种情况，就因为联邦调查局站在了另一端。
不管怎样，探员来到我的办公室，关上门，拿出他的徽章。“我得先给你看这个。”
这对我来说似乎很奇怪，因为我实际上没有任何理由相信他不是他所说的那个人，但某些工作会让你变得偏执，所以我对此不屑一顾。
在我们聊天的时候，我注意到他做了很多非常详细的笔记，这让我觉得很奇怪，但-再一次-我不屑一顾。这次谈话友好而富有成效。他适当地设定了期望。我们就以后如何跟进达成一致。他站起来离开，与我握手，转身走向门，但当他触摸门把手时，突然停了下来。
“哦，等等，我忘了一件事。你能举起你的右手一会儿吗？“。
“据你所知，你今天告诉我的一切都是真的？”
我看了看我仍然举起的右手。哦，……。。。“如果不是这样，我会坐牢一百万年，对吧？”
我头晕目眩，开始意识到我可能不小心咬了太多东西，最终我漫步回到USDS总部，这是白宫外拉斐特公园(Lafayette Park)附近的一座联排别墅。我在门厅碰到了马特·卡茨。
“最奇怪的事情刚刚发生了，”我在告诉他整个故事之前说。当我说完的时候，我意识到马特只是盯着我看，完全沉默。他那样盯着我看了好长时间。
“我有两百名员工，玛丽安。一直都是你。没有其他人会陷入这种情况。一直都是你。如果有人告诉我，我的一名雇员正在追捕联邦调查局的白人至上主义者，我就不会问是谁了，因为当然是你。一直都是你。为什么总是你呢？“
人们倾向于认为他们职业生涯中最大的决定因素是他们的才华或技能，但我不相信这一点。与其他任何单一因素相比，你处理风险和处理恐惧的方式对你从事哪种职业的影响更大。在USDS，人们看到我是一个大型猎人。少数几个人中的一个总是忙于真正关键的国家安全事务，但我没有追求那些项目，我只是抓住了我办公桌上出现的机会，其他人太害怕了，不敢采取行动。在联邦调查局的情况下，我之所以能做到这一点，很大程度上是因为我的无知。我没有停下来想，FBI的文化和程序可能会赋予我的外展以其他组织无法赋予的意义。但我也不认为自己承担了这个巨大的责任和所有相应的风险。我只是在那里联系两组人。为他们开门，这样他们就可以把事情做好。我并不担心我是否有资格或能力处理国内的白人至上主义者，因为我不认为这是我的职责。我相信研究人员会做好他们的工作。我相信联邦调查局会做好他们的工作。
让员工作为他们的经理表现出色，很大程度上是为了预测他们的决策，其中很大一部分是关于理解两件事：
但这不仅仅是管理者考虑他们的员工，在任何级别的组织中生存都需要预测其他人的行为。当人们开始担当起领导角色时，最终他们必须决定如何应对恐惧。当你是领导者时，你就是要为失败负责的人。你升得越高，你要承担的责任就越多，而你对它的控制就越少。信任你的团队很重要，但更重要的是你对风险的感知和接受。如果你更换了寻找值得信赖的员工，风险容忍度低的领导者就不会成为风险容忍度高的领导者。每个人都想拥有这种隐含的、本能的、don’t-need-to-think-about-it-and-never-doubt-it-for-a-second式的信任。这是黄金标准。但是，大多数信任关系的形成是因为你只是选择把你的怀疑和信任放在一边，而不是因为你有这些巨大的信仰蓄水池。
领导者在发展过程中倾向于在风险问题上朝着两个方向之一前进。有些领袖--就像武士一样--拥抱死亡。他们认为自己的角色是保护和授权那些在他们下面做真正工作的人，即使事情对他们个人不利，能为团队的利益服务也是一种荣誉。在最好的组织中，领导者的经理也不怕死，他们和他们给予员工的安全是一样的。
但有些人不会以这种方式处理风险。这些领导人被拉入试图逃脱死亡的境地，这在比喻上是不可能的，就像字面上说的一样。你不能永远逃避责任。最终会有一些失望或错误，你将被要求对此负责。
对风险容忍度低的领导者-我说领导者而不是经理，因为在工程团队中，这些概念也适用于员工和首席工程师-他们会通过直接避免风险或找出如何扭转局面，使之不是他们的错(TM)来应对风险。
这些领导人最终成为官僚。最完美的工作是拥有迷人的头衔和高薪的工作，在那里不需要做任何决定，也不需要冒任何风险。这些领导者最终进入初创公司的频率比你想象的要高。这是因为继续从事你一直在逃避死亡的职业会对你的心理造成真正的伤害。重要的是要明白，即使这些人看起来不在乎，他们仍然是人，他们和其他人一样，仍然有同样的基本需求和感受。逃脱死亡不仅伤害了他们扔在公交车下面的人，也伤害了他们。他们希望受到员工的尊重，但往往知道员工并不尊重他们。他们想要被钦佩，但往往知道自己不是。他们也觉得为了生存而欺骗别人也很内疚。
当你处在一个你受到尊重和钦佩的工作环境中时，这通常是它自己的回报。如果你没有这样的能力，你工作中给你价值感和满足感的部分都与职业阶梯直接相关。你的头衔是什么？您的项目的声望价值是多少？有多少人向你报到？有多少人向他们报到？你的工资是多少？你有多少股权？所有这些狗屎都变得非常重要，因为它往往是你的全部。逃脱死亡的人处于持续焦虑的状态。这就像是最糟糕的冒名顶替者综合症。他们通过攀登职业阶梯来自我安慰。因此，即使一家初创公司看起来不太适合，如果你不想承担责任，只想橡皮图章地
一旦人们开始以这种方式工作，就会形成一个不断升级的循环。没有人生来就知道如何管理一个1000人的工程组织。人们需要时间来培养技能，然后才能为升职做好准备。如果你的实际工作引发了足够的恐惧和焦虑，让你辜负了最亲密同事的信任，那么你不会做得更好。你越是超出你的能力范围，压力就越大，你需要跑得越快才能逃脱死亡。
最终，你会把所有的时间都花在努力让自己看起来很忙，逃避责任，通过找别人做替罪羊来挽回面子。与此同时，不怕死亡的领导者往往职业发展较慢，因为他们从实际工作中获得更多满足感，并专注于培养让自己变得更好的技能。这样做的最终结果往往是让人觉得高层的每个人都恶意无能。
note_en:
This is one of my favorite USDS stories: Very quickly in my career at USDS I developed a reputation for being a person you could send out an SOS distress call to, not so much inside USDS itself but externally with contractors and different government partners. There’s a fair amount of backstabbing in the government, even in the civil service. Whenever you chose to reach out to a 3rd party to ask for help you were taking a risk that the person you reached out to would screw you over. Just because DC doesn’t have a palace doesn’t mean we don’t have palace intrigues.
 Anyway, one day this group of security researchers reached out to me and let me know that the agency they were working with was being actively targeted by domestic white supremacists. They had alerted the appropriate people and made recommendations but were getting the run around. The website the white supremacists were interested in targeting had  thousands of known security vulnerabilities but it also had a very specific launch date non-negotiable and written into law (this is a stupidly common thing in government). Leadership did not want to stop development work to fix security issues, but they also could not be seen to be ignoring security issues. Especially not thousands of them. So they would engage with the researchers, put on this big performance with meetings and field trips and PowerPoint decks, and then do nothing.
 And they were doing this on multiple fronts, slow walking both external consultants and government oversight authorities. It wasn’t a situation where we could report the problem up to one of the many organizations in charge of information security in the federal government. Everyone who could be called upon to apply pressure was already aware of the situation and applying maximum pressure.
 I suggested we let the FBI know and see if they could block the potential attackers for us without worrying about the website. No crime had yet been committed, so the FBI couldn’t technically do anything about the threats. But they do track this kind of information and perhaps what the security researchers had could support other investigations. It took me a couple of weeks to find the right contact and reach out. After establishing that he was on the relevant task force and that they did track this info and were interested in hearing about what we had found, I suggested we go grab coffee and fill in some of the details. I had provided a set of potential locations, including the agency cafeteria which was super nice.
 “Actually do you have an office or a conference room we can use?”
 Sure, I said. And this didn’t bother me because the phrase  let’s get coffee is always kind of figurative. To begin with, I don’t drink coffee.
 A huge part of my job at USDS was coffee dates. The way we fought the bureaucracy was to build networks of people whose goals were aligned and whose skill sets or resources were compatible. The first contact with a new node was always informal. I needed to size you up, determine your incentives, your tolerance for risk, how you defined success. But I also needed to establish trust. I never thought to handle this situation any differently just because the FBI was on the other end of it.
 Anyway, the agent comes up to my office, he closes the door and takes out his badge. “I have to show you this first.”
 This seemed strange to me because I did not actually have any reason to believe he wasn’t who he said he was, but certain jobs will make you paranoid so I shrugged it off.
 As we chatted, I noticed he was taking a lot of very thorough notes, which struck me as odd but — again — I dismissed. The conversation was friendly and productive. He set expectations appropriately. We agreed on how to follow up later. He stood up to leave, shook my hand, turned for the door, but stopped short as he touched the door knob.
 “Oh wait, I forgot something. Could you raise your right hand for a minute?”
  “Everything you told me today is true to the best of your knowledge?”
 I looked at my still raised right hand.   Oh…….. “This is one of those things where if it isn’t I go to jail for a million years, right?”
    Dazed, starting to appreciate that I might have accidentally bitten off way more than I could chew, I eventually wandered back to USDS HQ, which is a townhouse off of Lafayette Park just outside the White House. I ran into Matt Cutts in the foyer.
  “The strangest thing just happened,” I said before telling him the whole story. When I finished I realized that Matt was just  staring at me, completely silent. He stared at me like that for an uncomfortably long time.
      “I have two hundred employees, Marianne. It’s always you. No one else gets into these situations.  It’s always you. If someone had told me that one of my employees was hunting white supremacists with the FBI I wouldn’t have to ask who it was because, of course it’s you. It’s always you. Why is it always you?”
 People tend to think that the biggest deciding factor in their career is their talent or skill, but I don’t believe that. The way you process risk and handle fear has more impact on what kind of career you have than any other single factor. At USDS people saw me a something of a big game hunter. One of those few people always tied up on really critical national security type things, but I didn’t pursue those projects I just took opportunities that came across my desk and that other people were too scared to act on. With the FBI situation a large part of my ability to do that was rooted in ignorance. I didn’t stop to think that the FBI’s culture and process might assign a significance to my outreach that other organizations would not. But I also didn’t see myself as taking on this huge responsibility and all the corresponding risks. I was just there to connect two groups of people. To open the door for them so that they could get things done. I didn’t worry about whether I was qualified or capable of handling domestic white supremacists because I didn’t see that as my role. I trusted the researchers to be good at their jobs. I trusted the FBI to be good at their jobs.
 A lot of getting people to perform well as their manager is about anticipating their decision making, and a lot of that is about understanding two things:
  But it’s not just about managers considering their employees, surviving in an organization at any level involves anticipating the behavior of others. When people begin to take on leadership roles eventually they have to decide how they are going to handle fear. When you are the leader, you are the person who is held accountable for failure. The higher up you go, the more you are accountable for and the less you have control over it. Trust in you team is important, but your perception and acceptance of risk even more so. Leaders with a low tolerance for risk do not become leaders with a high tolerance for risk if you change their employees looking for trustworthy ones. Everybody wants to have this implicit , instinctual, don’t-need-to-think-about-it-and-never-doubt-it-for-a-second trust. That’s the gold standard. But most relationships of trust are formed because you just choose to set aside your doubts and trust, not because you have these great reservoirs of faith.
 Leaders as they develop tend to break in one of two directions on risk. There are the leaders that — like samurai — embrace death. They see their role as protecting and empowering the people below them who do the real work and it’s an honor to serve the team’s interests even if things do not go well for them personally. In the best organizations the leader’s manager also does not fear death and they have the same safety as they grant to their people.
 But some people don’t process risk in that way. Those leaders get drawn into trying to outrun death, which is as impossible metaphorically as it would be literally. You can’t avoid taking responsibility forever. Eventually there will be some disappointment or mistake that you will be called on to answer for.
 The leaders who have a low tolerance for risk — and I say leaders instead of  managers because on engineering teams these concepts also apply to Staff and Principal engineers — will respond to risk by either avoiding it outright or figuring out how to spin the situation so that it is Not Their Fault(TM)
 These leaders ultimately become bureaucrats. The perfect job is the job with the glamorous title and the big paycheck where no decisions and no risks need to be taken. These leaders end up in startups more often than you might expect. That’s because continuing down a career where you’re running from death all the time does real damage to you psychologically. It’s important to understand that even though it seems like these people don’t care they are still human and they still have the same basic needs and feelings as anyone else. Fleeing death doesn’t just hurt the people they throw under the bus, it also hurts them. They want to be respected by their employees and often know they are not. They want to be admired and often know they are not. They also feel guilty screwing people over to survive.
 When you are in a work situation where you are respected and admired, that is often its own reward. If you don’t have that, the components of your job that give you a sense of value and satisfaction are all connected directly to the career ladder. What is your title? What is the prestige value of your projects? How many people report up to you? How many people report up to them? How much are you getting paid? How much equity do you have? All that shit becomes very important because it is often all you have. People who flee death are in a state of constant anxiety. It’s like the worst form of impostor syndrome. They self soothe by climbing the career ladder. So even though a startup may seem like a poor fit if you don’t want to take on responsibility and just want to rubber stamp things all day, those opportunities are very attractive to leaders who fear death because the prestige of them is a substitute for the satisfaction of doing a good job.
 And once people start working that way it becomes a cycle that escalates. No one is born knowing how to run a 1,000 person engineering organization. People need time to build skills before they are ready for a promotion. If your actual job triggers enough fear and anxiety for you to betray the trust of your closest colleagues, you won’t do better one level up. The further beyond your area of competence you get, the worse the stress gets, the faster you need to run to escape death.
 Eventually you end up spending all your time trying to look busy, avoid responsibility and save face by scapegoating others. Meanwhile leaders who don’t fear death tend to have slower career progressions because they take more satisfaction from their actual jobs and focus on building skills that will make them better. The end result of this is that it often feels like everyone at the top is maliciously incompetent.

title_en: Why is this idiot running my engineering org?

你刚上班，但是你有没有把烤箱留在家里呢？你老板发来的直截了当的“尽快给我回电话”的信息，但现在他们不接电话。或者，在那一刻，你意外地看到你的电脑上相机指示灯闪烁，你突然与一大堆你不认识的人进行了视频通话。
是的，最后一个是我。在我的辩护中，这只是我的轻微错误。
我得到了一条关于一家新的安全初创公司的消息，它有新的资金和一个引起我兴趣的想法。我没有太多可做的，所以我做了任何好奇的记者都做过的事情，开始四处挖掘。这家创业公司的网站引人注目，但主要是文字沙拉。我找不到简单问题的基本答案。但该公司的想法看起来仍然很明智。我只想知道公司到底是怎么运作的。
记者使用大量的工具来收集信息，监控网站的变化，检查是否有人打开了他们的电子邮件进行评论，以及浏览海量的公共数据池。这些工具并不是特别的，只为媒体的持卡人保留，而是对任何想要查找和报道信息的人开放。我在安全节拍上经常使用的一个工具列出了公司网站上的所有子域名。这些子域是公共的，但故意隐藏起来，但你经常可以从网站本身找到你找不到的东西。
对啰!。我立刻找到了公司的摊位甲板。另一个子域有大量关于其产品工作原理的文档。有几个子域没有加载，只有几个子域被员工屏蔽了。(这也是法律沙子里的一条线。如果不是公开的，不让你进去，你就不能敲门。)。
我点击了另一个子域。页面一下子打开了，我的Mac底座上的一个图标短暂地弹了一下，相机的灯也亮了起来。我还没来得及登记正在发生的事情，我就参加了似乎是公司的晨会。
唯一得救的是我的网络摄像头盖，一个专有的自制双层遮蔽带，它阻止了似乎有六个人盯着我看，以及我衣衫褴褛、被流行病驱使的外表。
我没有留下来为自己解释，但很快就给公司发了电子邮件，警告安全漏洞。该公司已经将他们的Zoom会议室硬编码到他们公司网站上的一些子域中。任何知道这个容易猜到的子域的人--相信我，你都能猜到--都会立即参加该公司的一次站起来的Zoom会议。不需要密码。
Zoom也遇到了安全问题，并被迫更改默认设置以防止滥用，这在很大程度上是由于对该平台的更严格审查，因为自冠状病毒大流行开始以来，该平台的使用率飙升。
但这不在Zoom上，这次没有。这家公司将一个完全没有保护措施的Zoom会议室连接到一个方便记忆的网址，这可能是为了方便，但这个地址可能会让潜伏者和窃听者留在公司的会议上。
对你的Zoom会议进行密码保护要求不多，因为下一次可能不会是我。
note_en:
You just got to work but did you leave the oven on at home? The gut-punch “call me ASAP” message from your boss but now they’re not answering their phone. Or that moment you unexpectedly see your camera light flash on your computer and you’re suddenly in a video call with a ton of people you don’t know.
 Yes, that last one was me. In my defense it was only slightly my fault.
 I got a tip about a new security startup, with fresh funding and an idea that caught my interest. I didn’t have much to go on, so I did what any curious reporter did and started digging around. The startup’s website was splashy, but largely word salad. I couldn’t find basic answers to my simple questions. But the company’s idea still seemed smart. I just wanted to know how the company actually worked.
  Reporters use a ton of tools to collect information, monitor changes in websites, check if someone opened their email for comment, and to navigate vast pools of public data. These tools aren’t special, reserved only for card-carrying members of the press, but rather open to anyone who wants to find and report information. One tool I use frequently on the security beat lists all the subdomains on a company’s website. These subdomains are public but deliberately hidden from view, yet you can often find things that you wouldn’t from the website itself.
 Bingo! I immediately found the company’s pitch deck. Another subdomain had a ton of documentation on how its product works. A bunch of subdomains didn’t load, and a couple were blocked off for employees only. (It’s also a line in the legal sand. If it’s not public and you’re not allowed in, you’re not allowed to knock down the door.)
 I clicked on another subdomain. A page flashed open, an icon in my Mac dock briefly bounced, and the camera light flashed on. Before I could register what was happening, I had joined what appeared to be the company’s morning meeting.
 The only saving grace was my webcam cover, a proprietary home-made double layer of masking tape that blocked what looked like half a dozen people from staring back at me and my unkempt, pandemic-driven appearance.
 I didn’t stick around to explain myself, but quickly emailed the company to warn of the security lapse. The company had hardcoded their Zoom meeting rooms to a number of subdomains on their company’s website. Anyone who knew the easy-to-guess subdomain — trust me, you could guess it — would immediately launch into one of the company’s standing Zoom meetings. No password required.
  Zoom has seen  its share of security issues and  forced to change default settings to prevent abuse, largely driven by greater scrutiny of the platform as  its usage rocketed since the start of the coronavirus pandemic.
 But this wasn’t on Zoom, not this time. This was a company that connected an entirely unprotected Zoom meeting room to a conveniently memorable web address, likely for convenience, but one that could have left lurkers and eavesdroppers in the company’s meetings.
 It’s not much to ask to password-protect your Zoom meetings, because next time it  probably won’t be me.
title: 我是如何不小心破坏了一家初创公司的晨会
title_en: How I accidentally gatecrashed a startup’s morning meeting

note:
一段时间以来，Mozilla一直在为谷歌的Android操作系统开发一款新的移动浏览器。他们的想法是从零开始创建一个浏览器，使用现代框架和技术来与Android上的Chrome等竞争。
这款浏览器的晚间版和测试版已经有一段时间了，最近才作为稳定版本发布。阶段性迁移已经开始，在他们的设备上安装了Firefox的Android用户很快就会注意到，如果他们允许升级，浏览器就会升级到新的浏览器。
新浏览器提供了更好的性能和网页兼容性，但用户可能也会注意到，旧的安卓火狐浏览器支持的一些功能是不可用的。新用户，那些从未使用过Firefox for Android的人，可能会喜欢这款浏览器，因为它速度快，提供了良好的网络兼容性，并包括一系列扩展，包括流行的内容拦截程序uBlock Origin。
以下是现有Firefox for Android用户可能想要立即阻止更新到新版本的三个原因：
归根结底总会是这样的。Mozilla有过早发布更改的习惯，Firefox for Android的发布就是另一个例子。Classic Firefox for Android支持该浏览器的大多数扩展，在撰写本文时，新版本只支持9个。
除九个受支持的扩展外，所有已安装的扩展在升级期间都将被禁用。它们并没有消失，而且Mozilla承诺新的Firefox最终将支持所有浏览器扩展。不过，该组织没有给出任何时间表，这是一个主要问题。
九个扩展中有三个是内容拦截程序，包括最流行的uBlock Origin，这应该可以满足许多用户的需求，但那些依赖密码管理器、新标签页定制程序、其他安全扩展、下载管理器、媒体扩展和其他的人现在无法使用这些功能。例如，如果浏览器不支持扩展，您应该如何在浏览器中使用密码管理器密码。Firefox for Android包括一个内置的密码管理器--顺时针，但并不是每个人都想移植到它上，也不是每个人都知道如何移植。
从用户的角度来看，这一点特别成问题的是，目前还不清楚新浏览器何时可以提供对某些扩展的支持(如果有的话)。
稳定版Firefox for Android的高级配置界面是关于：Config关闭的。你可以在测试版和夜间版中使用它，因为这两个版本都可以使用，但许多用户可能不想仅仅为了在他们的设备上运行一个不稳定的版本。
关于：Config提供了一个高级选项宝库，这些选项是通过浏览器的设置无法使用的。Firefox用户使用它来加强浏览器，或更改某些无法在设置中更改的功能。最近的例子包括启用重定向跟踪保护、HTTP/3支持或启用新的仅HTTPS模式。
Mozilla认为，某些配置条目中的一个错误值就可能破坏整个浏览器。对于使用about：config的用户来说，这无疑是一种倒退，而对于某些用户来说，它可能是一场精彩的表演。
Firefox附带了一组搜索提供商，例如Google、DuckDuckGo和Bing，你可以在它们之间切换。它包括添加自定义提供程序的选项，并可能在页面上列出一些提供程序。将传统Firefox升级到新版本时，不会迁移现有的搜索提供程序。
添加搜索提供商过去非常容易，但在Firefox for Android中不再容易。选择设置&gt；搜索&gt；添加搜索引擎&gt；其他以使表单能够将自定义搜索提供程序添加到新的Firefox for Android。
问题是，您必须在表单的字段中手动键入搜索字符串。因为它需要一个变量，所以复制和粘贴不能自动工作，这是一个大问题。想要添加Start Page Search或任何其他官方不支持的搜索引擎吗？您需要运行搜索，用变量替换搜索词，然后将所有内容复制到Firefox界面，或者在将原始URL粘贴到Firefox设置中的表单后对其进行编辑。
Mozilla的大部分收入来自搜索引擎交易，从伦理的角度来看，在迁移期间恢复默认搜索引擎而忽略自定义搜索引擎的决定是有问题的。
新的安卓版火狐将取悦那些过去使用过没有扩展的前身浏览器的用户，比如：配置调整，以及使用标准搜索引擎。对浏览器进行的修改越少越好，因为这些用户将获得更快、更可靠的浏览器。首次在Android上安装Firefox的新用户可能也会喜欢这款浏览器。
然而，使用扩展或about：config调整的Firefox用户将会失望，因为Mozilla将Firefox相对于Chrome的两个最大优势从稳定版本中移除。
广告
note_en:
Mozilla has been working on a new mobile browser for Google's Android operating system for some time now. The idea was to create a browser from scratch that uses modern frameworks and technologies to compete with the likes of Chrome on Android.
 The browser has been available as Nightly and Beta channel versions for some time, and has just recently been released as a Stable version.  Staged migration has begun and Android users who have installed Firefox on their devices will soon notice that the browser is upgraded to the new one provided that they allow the upgrade.
 The new browser offers better performance and web compatibility, but users may also notice that some features are not available that the old Firefox browser for Android supported. New users, those who never used Firefox for Android, may like the browser as it is fast, offers good web compatibility, and includes a selection of extensions including the popular content blocker uBlock Origin.
 Here are three reasons why existing Firefox for Android users may want to block the update to the new version right now:
   It always comes down to this. Mozilla has a habit of releasing changes too early, and the release of Firefox for Android is another example of that. Classic Firefox for Android supported most extensions for the browser, the new version only nine at the time of writing.
 All installed extensions, except for the nine supported, will get disabled during the upgrade. They are not gone, and Mozilla has promised that the new Firefox will support all browser extensions eventually. The organization has not given any time frame though for that, and that is a major problem.
 Three of the nine extensions are content blockers, including the most popular of them all, uBlock Origin, and that should cover the needs of many users, but those who rely on password managers, New Tab page customizers, other security extensions, download managers, media extensions and others, won't be able to use these right now. For example, how are you supposed to use your password manager passwords in the browser if it does not support the extension. Firefox for Android includes Lockwise, a password manager that is built-in, but not everyone wants to migrate to it or knows how.
 What makes this particularly problematic from a user point of view is that it is unclear if any when support for certain extensions will become available in the new browser.
   The stable version of Firefox for Android has the advanced configuration interface about:config disabled. You can use it in Beta and Nightly versions, as it is available in both, but many users may not want to run an unstable version just for that on their device.
 About:config offers a treasure trove of advanced options that are not available via the browser's settings. Firefox users use it to harden the browser, or change certain features that cannot be changed in the settings. Recent examples include enabling  redirect tracking protection,  HTTP/3 support, or enabling the new  HTTPS-only mode.
 Mozilla  argues that a single wrong value in some configuration entries can break the entire browser. It is certainly a regression for users who use about:config and for some, it may be a showstopper.
   Firefox ships with a set of search providers, e.g. Google, DuckDuckGo and Bing, that you may switch between. It includes options to add custom providers, and may list some on the page. Existing search providers are not migrated when the classic Firefox is upgraded to the new.
 Adding search providers used to be very easy, but it is no longer easy in Firefox for Android. Select Settings > Search > Add Search Engine > Other to enable the form to add a custom search provider to the new Firefox for Android.
 Problem is, you have to type the search string manually into a field of the form. Since it requires a variable, copy and paste is not working automatically and that is a big problem. Want to add Startpage Search or any other search engine not supported officially? You need to run a search, replace the search term with the variable, and copy all that to the Firefox interface, or edit the original URL after you pasted it into the form in the Firefox Settings.
 Mozilla makes most of its revenue from search engine deals and the decision to restore the default search engine during the migration and to ignore custom search engines, is problematic from an ethical point of view.
  The new Firefox for Android will please users who used the browser's predecessor in the past without extensions, about:config tweaks, and with the standard search engine. The less modifications done to the browser the better as these users will get a faster more reliable browser. New users who install Firefox on Android for the first time may also like the browser.
 Firefox users who used extensions or about:config tweaks however, will be disappointed as Mozilla removed two of the biggest advantages of Firefox over Chrome from the stable version.
